title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: Dimensions 25 datasets regdatasets.
code: library(regdatasets)  # Build summary table datasets <- c(   "alcohol1_pp", "berk_sub", "berkeley", "civic_ed", "classdata_07",   "crime", "disc", "disc2", "faculty", "gcse", "grades", "gss_1",   "hsb_sub", "hsbs1", "individuals", "instruction", "lambert",   "nels_data", "penalty", "pisa2000", "reading",   "satisfaction", "titanic", "womenlf" )  summary_df <- data.frame(   Dataset = datasets,   Rows = integer(length(datasets)),   Cols = integer(length(datasets)),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  for (i in seq_along(datasets)) {   data(list = datasets[i], package = "regdatasets")   df <- get(datasets[i])   summary_df$Rows[i] <- nrow(df)   summary_df$Cols[i] <- ncol(df) }  knitr::kable(summary_df, format = "html",              caption = "Dimensions of all 25 datasets in regdatasets.",              col.names = c("Dataset", "Observations", "Variables"))

title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: table shows datasets used chapter companion textbook.

title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: datasets support core linear regression chapters (1-8): gcse (4,059 x 9): primary running example. GCSE exam scores London Reading Test scores 65 schools. Used across Chapters 1, 2, 5 SLR, ANCOVA, interactions. crime (67 x 5): Florida county-level crime rates. Demonstrates confounding/sign-reversal phenomenon education crime urbanization controlled. instruction (1,190 x 12): Math achievement gains nested classrooms schools. Supports ANOVA ANCOVA demonstrations. reading (66 x 8): Three-group reading instruction experiment. Clean example one-way ANOVA clear group differences.

title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: datasets support binary outcome chapters (9-11): berkeley (3,593 x 3): classic Simpson’s paradox dataset. UC Berkeley graduate admissions gender department. gss_1 (2,832 x 16): General Social Survey 1982/1994. Attitude change time simple logistic regression example. penalty (674 x 3): Death penalty sentencing data defendant victim race. Used goodness--fit testing. titanic (1,309 x 6): Passenger survival data class, sex, age, fare predictors.

title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: womenlf (263 x 5): Canadian women’s labor force participation three-level ordinal outcome. satisfaction (110 x 3): Four-level ordinal satisfaction data ordinal logistic regression. lambert (492 x 109): Rich longitudinal dataset latent response model demonstrations.

title: Dataset Overview and Course Mapping
text: datasets originate published educational social science studies. Key sources include: GCSE data: Goldstein et al. (1993), UK examination council records PISA 2000: OECD Programme International Student Assessment Berkeley admissions: Bickel, Hammel & O’Connell (1975) General Social Survey: NORC University Chicago NAEP: National Center Education Statistics NELS:88: National Education Longitudinal Study 1988 Titanic: British Board Trade records See individual dataset documentation (?dataset_name) complete source citations.

title: Getting Started with regdatasets
text: gcse dataset primary running example Chapters 1–2. contains GCSE exam scores London Reading Test (LRT) scores 4,059 students 65 London secondary schools: GCSE scores vs. LRT scores fitted regression line.
code: # Fit a simple linear regression model <- lm(gcse ~ lrt, data = gcse) summary(model) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = gcse ~ lrt, data = gcse) #>  #> Residuals: #>      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max  #> -26.5617  -5.1847   0.1265   5.4397  29.7399  #>  #> Coefficients: #>             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept) -0.01195    0.12642  -0.095    0.925     #> lrt          0.59506    0.01273  46.744   <2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 8.054 on 4057 degrees of freedom #> Multiple R-squared:   0.35,  Adjusted R-squared:  0.3499  #> F-statistic:  2185 on 1 and 4057 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16 library(ggplot2)  ggplot(gcse, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#E74C3C", se = TRUE) +   labs(     title = "GCSE vs. London Reading Test",     x = "London Reading Test Score (Age 11)",     y = "GCSE Score (Age 16)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Getting Started with regdatasets
text: berkeley dataset demonstrates Simpson’s paradox — reversal apparent gender bias controlling department: crude odds ratio suggests female applicants disadvantaged, adjusting department effect essentially disappears — classic example confounding.
code: data(berkeley)  # Unadjusted model crude <- glm(admitted ~ female, family = binomial, data = berkeley)  # Adjusted model (controlling for department) adjusted <- glm(admitted ~ female + factor(department),                 family = binomial, data = berkeley)  # Compare odds ratios exp(coef(crude)["female"])    # Crude OR #>    female  #> 0.6394079 exp(coef(adjusted)["female"]) # Adjusted OR #>    female  #> 0.9697744

title: Getting Started with regdatasets
text: reading dataset compares three reading instruction methods: Post-test scores instruction group.
code: data(reading)  # ANOVA via regression with dummy variables model_anova <- lm(post1 ~ factor(group), data = reading) anova(model_anova) #> Analysis of Variance Table #>  #> Response: post1 #>               Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value   Pr(>F)    #> factor(group)  2 108.12  54.061  5.3174 0.007347 ** #> Residuals     63 640.50  10.167                     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ggplot(reading, aes(x = factor(group), y = post1, fill = factor(group))) +   geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("#2C3E50", "#E74C3C", "#3498DB")) +   labs(     title = "Reading Comprehension by Instruction Method",     x = "Instruction Group",     y = "Post-test Score (post1)"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: regdatasets: Datasets for Teaching Regression Analysis
code: library(regdatasets)  # --- Simple linear regression --- data(gcse) model <- lm(gcse ~ lrt, data = gcse) summary(model)  # --- Logistic regression: Simpson's paradox --- data(berkeley) # Unadjusted: gender appears to matter crude <- glm(admitted ~ female, family = binomial, data = berkeley) # Adjusted: gender effect disappears after controlling for department adjusted <- glm(admitted ~ female + factor(department),                 family = binomial, data = berkeley) exp(coef(crude)["female"])    # Crude OR ~ 0.64 exp(coef(adjusted)["female"]) # Adjusted OR ~ 0.97  # --- One-way ANOVA --- data(reading) model_anova <- lm(post1 ~ factor(group), data = reading) anova(model_anova)

title: regdatasets: Datasets for Teaching Regression Analysis
text: regdatasets designed work seamlessly modern R workflows: Example using easystats marginaleffects ecosystem:
code: library(tidyverse)   # dplyr, ggplot2, tibble, ... library(easystats)   # parameters, performance, effectsize, see, ... library(gtsummary)   # publication-ready tables library(marginaleffects) # predictions, comparisons, slopes library(regdatasets) library(easystats) library(marginaleffects)  # Fit model data(gcse) model <- lm(gcse ~ lrt * school, data = gcse)  # easystats: model summary and diagnostics model_parameters(model) check_model(model)  # marginaleffects: predicted values and slopes plot_predictions(model, condition = c("lrt", "school")) avg_slopes(model, variables = "lrt", by = "school")

what: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
title: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
text: Longitudinal person-period data study adolescent alcohol use, tracking 82 adolescents age 14 age 16. row represents one person one time point, resulting 246 observations (82 persons x 3 occasions). data include measures alcohol consumption, peer alcohol use, whether adolescent child alcoholic (COA). dataset commonly used multilevel/growth curve modeling textbooks illustrate individual growth trajectories effects time-varying time-invariant predictors.

title: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
code: alcohol1_pp

title: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
text: tibble 246 rows 9 columns: id Person identifier. Type: numeric. Range: (1, 82). Uniquely identifies 82 adolescents. age Age years time measurement. Type: numeric. Values: 14, 15, 16. Three measurement occasions. coa Child alcoholic. Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = child alcoholic parent, 0 = child alcoholic. Time-invariant predictor. male Sex adolescent. Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = male, 0 = female. Time-invariant predictor. age_14 Age centered 14 (.e., age minus 14). Type: numeric. Values: 0, 1, 2. Used time variable growth models intercept represents alcohol use age 14. alcuse Alcohol use composite score. Type: numeric. Range: (0.00, 3.61). Higher values indicate greater alcohol consumption. primary outcome variable. peer Peer alcohol use score. Type: numeric. Range: (0.00, 2.53). Mean = 1.02. Higher values indicate greater peer alcohol use. Time-varying covariate. cpeer Peer alcohol use, grand-mean centered. Type: numeric. Range: (-1.02, 1.51). Mean approximately 0. peer variable centered subtracting grand mean (1.02) aid interpretation intercept regression models. ccoa Child alcoholic, grand-mean centered. Type: numeric. Values: -0.45, 0.55. Mean approximately 0. COA variable centered subtracting grand mean (0.45) intercept represents expected outcome average person predictor rather non-COA.

title: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
text: Singer, J. D., & Willett, J. B. (2003). Applied Longitudinal Data Analysis: Modeling Change Event Occurrence. Oxford University Press. Original data file: alcohol1_pp.dta

title: Adolescent Alcohol Use Person-Period Data — alcohol1_pp
text: dataset used Chapters interactions multilevel modeling illustrate growth curve models person-period data structures. Key analyses include: fitting individual growth curves alcohol use time, examining effects COA status peer influence alcohol use trajectories, demonstrating grand-mean centering predictors. centered variables (cpeer, ccoa) illustrate common practice centering predictors improve interpretability intercept multilevel longitudinal models. Grand-mean centering shifts meaning intercept value predictors zero value sample average predictor.

Overall admission rate 44%. male Sex applicant. Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = male, 0 = female. Approximately 64% applicants male. engineering Department applied . Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = Engineering, 0 = Psychology. two departments differ substantially gender composition admission rates.

title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions Subset (Engineering and Psychology) — berk_sub
text: Bickel, P. J., Hammel, E. ., & O'Connell, J. W. (1975). Sex bias graduate admissions: Data Berkeley. Science, 187(4175), 398-404. Original data file: berk_sub.dta

title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions Subset (Engineering and Psychology) — berk_sub
text: dataset used Chapter 10 (Multiple Logistic Regression) illustrate confounding omitted variable bias logistic regression. Key analyses include: computing unadjusted odds ratios admission gender, fitting multiple logistic regression controlling department, demonstrating apparent gender effect (unadjusted = 3.25) largely disappears adjusting department (adjusted approximately 1.1). Engineering department much higher proportion male applicants (approximately 96%) compared Psychology (approximately 33%), also higher overall admission rate (approximately 63%) Psychology (approximately 25%). creates conditions Simpson's paradox: omitted variable (department) correlated predictor (gender) predictor outcome (admission).

title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions Data (Five Departments) — berkeley
text: dataset used Chapters 10-11 (Multiple Logistic Regression Model Fit/Diagnostics) illustrate confounding logistic regression Simpson's paradox. Key analyses include: computing odds ratios admission gender within across departmentnts, fitting multiple logistic regression models departmentnt control variable, performing likelihood ratio tests, chi-squared goodness--fit tests. what: Civic Education Study: Pre-Post Survey Data — civic_ed
title: Civic Education Study: Pre-Post Survey Data — civic_ed
text: Pre-test post-test survey data civic education study involving 794 students across 16 schools. study measured wide range civic attitudes, knowledge, behaviors, participation civic education intervention. 237 variables covering survey items (Likert scales, binary, open-ended), composite scale scores, demographic information, dataset supports analyses treatment effects, regression mean, scale construction.

title: Civic Education Study: Pre-Post Survey Data — civic_ed
code: civic_ed

title: Civic Education Study: Pre-Post Survey Data — civic_ed
text: tibble 794 rows 237 columns. Variables organized following groups: Identifiers treatment indicators: id_match Unique student identifier matching pre post records. Type: numeric. Range: (201180001, 351190031). school School identifier. Type: character. 16 unique schools coded : 20, 21, 22, 23, 25, 26, 27, 29, others. wtp People program participation. Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = participated civic education program, 0 = comparison group. 63% participated. ap AP (Advanced Placement) class indicator. Type: numeric. Binary indicator (0/1) 1 = AP class, 0 = non-AP class. Pre-test survey items (t_q1 t_q99): Variables prefixed t_ represent pre-test (Time 1) survey responses. include: t_q1 t_q23 Civic knowledge attitude items. Type: numeric. Mostly 4-point Likert scales (1-4). items 3-point scales (1-3). Small amounts missing data (NA = 0 8). t_numorg Number organizations participated (pre-test). Type: numeric. Range: (0, 5). t_orgs_y organization membership (pre-test). Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) 1 = member least one organization. t_q24 Political interest item. Type: numeric. 6-point scale (1-6). t_q25 t_q28 Political activity items (pre-test). Type: numeric. Binary (0/1). t_numldr Number leadership roles (pre-test). Type: numeric. Range: (0, 4). t_q29 t_q35 Civic activity items (pre-test). Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) missing values. t_numact Number civic activities (pre-test). Type: numeric. Range: (0, 7). t_q36 t_q47 Civic attitude items. Type: numeric. 4-point Likert scales (1-4). t_q48, t_q49, t_q50 Knowledge test items. Type: character. Multiple choice responses (, B, C, D, E, F) -1 indicating missing response. t_q51 t_q52 Attitude items. Type: numeric. 4-point scales (1-4). t_q53 t_q64 Political feeling thermometer items. Type: numeric. 7-point scales (1-7). Higher values indicate warmer feelings. t_q65 t_q78 Civic engagement obligation items. Type: numeric. 4-point Likert scales (1-4). t_q79 t_q84 Additional feeling thermometer items. Type: numeric. 7-point scales (1-7). t_q85 t_q89 Tolerance ambiguity items. Type: numeric. 4- 5-point scales. t_q92_a t_q92_f Race/ethnicity indicators (pre-test). Type: numeric. Binary (0/1). Multiple categories (e.g., = Asian, b = Black, c = Hispanic, etc.). t_q90, t_q91 Media exposure items. Type: numeric. 6-point scales (0-5). t_q93 Political discussion home. Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) 1 = discusses politics home. t_q94 Religious attendance. Type: numeric. Range: (0, 3). t_q95 Self-reported grades. Type: numeric. Range: (1, 8). t_q96_a t_q96_k Subject enrollment indicators. Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) various courses taken. t_q97 t_q99 Additional survey items. Type: numeric. 4- 5-point scales. Post-test survey items (r_q1 r_q84): Variables prefixed r_ represent post-test (Time 2) survey responses. mirror pre-test items numbering (e.g., r_q1 corresponds t_q1). Scales coding identical pre-test versions. Demographic background variables: par_coll Parent college education. Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) 1 = least one parent attended college. NA = 40. hi_math Highest math course taken. Type: numeric. Range: (0, 5). Higher values indicate advanced math courses. Composite scale scores (standardized, mean approximately 0): Variables ending _m IRT-based factor-analytic composite scores. Suffixes _t _r indicate pre-test post-test respectively. attn_pre, attn_pos Attention politics (pre post). Type: numeric. Type: numeric. Range: (0.17, 80.00). 263 missing values. Fractional ages (e.g., 0.17) represent infants. fare Passenger fare British pounds. Type: numeric. Range: (0.00, 512.33). 1 missing value. Varies substantially class. embarked Port embarkation. Type: character. Values: S = Southampton, C = Cherbourg, Q = Queenstown (Cobh, Ireland). title: Titanic Passenger Survival Data — titanic
text: dataset used Chapters 9-11 (Logistic Regression) illustrate simple multiple logistic regression modeling. well-known "women children first" evacuation policy makes dataset effective demonstrating sex, class, age predict binary outcomes. title: Canadian Women's Labor Force Participation — womenlf
text: Data labor force participation 263 married Canadian women, including work status (working, part-time, full-time), husband's income, region residence, presence children. dataset classic example ordinal response modeling, three-level work status variable represents ordered outcome ranging labor force participation full-time employment.

title: Canadian Women's Labor Force Participation — womenlf

title: Canadian Women's Labor Force Participation — womenlf
text: tibble 263 rows 5 columns: obs Observation number. Type: numeric. Range: (1, 263). husbinc Husband's annual income thousands Canadian dollars. Type: numeric. Range: (1, 45). Mean approximately $14,760. region Canadian region residence. Type: character. Levels: "Atlantic" = Atlantic provinces, "Quebec" = Quebec, "Ontario" = Ontario, "Prairie" = Prairie provinces, "BC" = British Columbia. workstat Work status (ordinal response variable). Type: numeric. Values: 0 = working outside home, 1 = working part-time, 2 = working full-time. Ordered lowest highest labor force participation. chilpres Presence children household. Type: numeric. Binary (0/1) 1 = children present, 0 = children present.
